title: Diabetes Mellitus: Management and Therapies
authors: Alvin C. Powers, Kevin D. Niswender, Michael R. Rickels

items.title: Individualizing the HbA1c target
items.content:
The ADA goal is an HbA1c as close to normal as possible without significant hypoglycemia. For most adults the target is <7% (some patients ≤6.5%). A HIGHER target of <7.5–8% is appropriate for those with cognitive impairment, reduced ability to sense hypoglycemia, or limited life span.

Approximately 1 in 4 people over 65 has diabetes; in a robust elderly patient the target may be the same as for younger adults, but in someone with poor health/cognitive impairment or in long-term care the priority shifts to avoiding hypoglycemia and severe hyperglycemia.
keyPoints: Most adults: HbA1c <7%; some <6.5%.
Relax to <7.5–8% for hypoglycemia risk/cognitive impairment/limited life span.
In high-CV-risk/ASCVD populations, avoid hypoglycemia — near-normal glycemia is not the goal.
items.reference: Glycemic Control: "In most individuals, the target HbA1c should be <7%... A higher HbA1c target of <7.5 or 8% is appropriate for individuals with cognitive impairment, those with reduced ability to sense hypoglycemia, or those with limited life span."

items.title: Why HbA1c reflects 2–3 months and when it misleads
items.content:
Persistent hyperglycemia non-enzymatically glycates hemoglobin; because red cells live ~120 days, HbA1c reflects average glycemia over the prior 2–3 months (the most recent month contributes ~50%). Measure ≥2×/year at goal, every 3 months when control is inadequate or therapy changes.

HbA1c is unreliable whenever red-cell turnover or hemoglobin is abnormal: hemoglobinopathies, anemias, reticulocytosis, transfusion, uremia/hemodialysis, EPO therapy, and G6PD variants. (HbA1c also runs ~0.3% higher in African Americans for the same mean glucose.) In these settings, use CGM/glucose or fructosamine/glycated albumin (2–4 week window).
keyPoints: HbA1c = 2–3 month average; last month ≈ 50%.
Unreliable with altered RBC lifespan/hemoglobin (hemoglobinopathy, anemia, hemodialysis, EPO).
Alternatives: CGM, fructosamine/glycated albumin.
items.reference: Long-Term Glycemic Control: "this alteration reflects the glycemic history over the previous 2–3 months, because erythrocytes have an average life span of ~120 days... Clinical conditions leading to abnormal red blood cell parameters such as hemoglobinopathies, anemias, reticulocytosis, transfusions, uremia... may alter the HbA1c result."

items.title: Why rapid-acting analogues are preferred over regular insulin for meals
items.content:
Rapid-acting analogues (lispro, aspart, glulisine) have amino-acid modifications that reduce self-aggregation, giving faster absorption/onset and a SHORTER duration. This lets the insulin's rise and fall track the post-meal glucose curve — so they are injected just before (<10 min) a meal, whereas regular insulin must be given 30–45 min ahead.

The shorter action also means fewer hypoglycemic episodes, because insulin action decays as the post-meal glucose falls (regular insulin lingers and can cause late hypoglycemia).
keyPoints: Analogues: faster on/off → dose just before the meal.
Regular insulin: give 30–45 min before the meal.
Shorter action → fewer late hypoglycemic episodes.
items.reference: Insulin Preparations: "insulin aspart, lispro, or glulisine is preferred over regular insulin for prandial coverage... The shorter duration of action also appears to be associated with a decreased number of hypoglycemic episodes."

items.title: Long-acting basal insulins and nocturnal hypoglycemia
items.content:
Basal insulin fine-tunes hepatic glucose output (glycogenolysis/gluconeogenesis) and suppresses lipolysis/ketogenesis. Glargine forms microprecipitates at physiologic pH, giving a later onset, ~24-h duration, and a less pronounced peak than NPH — with LESS nocturnal hypoglycemia. Degludec forms multihexamers and binds albumin, lasting >42 h with even fewer nocturnal/severe hypoglycemic events.

Type 1 patients need 0.4–1.0 U/kg/day, with 30–50% given as basal and the rest prandial. Every patient with type 1 DM should also have a filled glucagon prescription.
keyPoints: Glargine/degludec: peakless, long-acting, less nocturnal hypoglycemia than NPH.
Type 1: 0.4–1.0 U/kg/day, ~30–50% basal.
All type 1 patients need glucagon on hand.
items.reference: Insulin Preparations: "A lower incidence of hypoglycemia, especially at night, has been reported with insulin glargine when compared to NPH insulin... insulin degludec... provides similar glycemic control as glargine but with less frequent nocturnal and severe hypoglycemia."

items.title: How insulin-to-carb ratio and correction factor work
items.content:
In intensive (basal-bolus) therapy the mealtime dose has two parts: (1) a MEAL component from the insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio (commonly ~1 unit per 10–15 g carbohydrate, individualized), plus (2) a CORRECTION component for the current glucose (e.g., 1 unit per 30–60 mg/dL above target; the correction factor can be estimated as 1500 ÷ total daily dose).

The basal (long-acting) dose is titrated to the fasting glucose. CGM or frequent BGM is essential for these calculations. Fixed combination insulins are convenient but cannot independently adjust rapid- and long-acting components, so they are NOT appropriate for type 1 DM.
keyPoints: Meal dose = carbs ÷ insulin-to-carb ratio; add correction for glucose above target.
Correction factor ≈ 1500 ÷ total daily insulin dose.
Premixed combinations can't be independently adjusted → not for type 1 DM.
items.reference: Insulin Regimens: "the patient uses an insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio (a common ratio for type 1 DM is 1 unit/10–15 g of carbohydrate)... this correction factor can be estimated from 1500/[total daily insulin dose]."

items.title: Why a disconnected insulin pump rapidly causes DKA
items.content:
Pumps (CSII/automated insulin delivery) use only rapid-acting analogues (lispro, aspart, glulisine), which have a very short half-life. There is NO basal long-acting depot — so if the infusion set occludes or the pump disconnects, insulin levels fall within hours and the patient can develop ketosis/DKA quickly.

This is why pump users need education, CGM/frequent BGM, and a backup plan to inject long- and/or rapid-acting insulin if the device fails. Automated insulin delivery (AID — pump + CGM + algorithm) is the preferred delivery method for most type 1 patients but isn't fully closed-loop (the patient still enters carbs/exercise).
keyPoints: Pumps use short-half-life rapid insulin only → interruption → fast insulin deficiency/DKA.
Always have a backup injection plan.
AID (pump+CGM+algorithm) is preferred for most type 1 patients.
items.reference: Insulin pumps: "the short half-life of these insulins quickly leads to insulin deficiency if the delivery system is interrupted... DKA if the insulin infusion device becomes disconnected."

items.title: Why metformin is first-line — and when to stop it
items.content:
Metformin (a biguanide) reduces hepatic glucose production (and slightly improves peripheral utilization), lowers fasting glucose and insulin, improves lipids, promotes mild WEIGHT LOSS, does NOT cause hypoglycemia, and is low-cost — hence first-line in every major guideline. Titrate slowly to 2000 mg/day to limit GI effects; monitor vitamin B12 (levels fall).

Its feared toxicity, lactic acidosis, is very rare but prevented by patient selection: AVOID/STOP metformin with GFR <30, any acidosis, unstable CHF, liver disease, severe hypoxemia, in hospitalized/NPO patients, and before iodinated contrast — use insulin until it can be restarted.
keyPoints: First-line: effective, weight-neutral/loss, no hypoglycemia, cheap.
Monitor B12; titrate slowly for GI tolerance.
Hold for GFR <30, acidosis, unstable CHF, liver disease, contrast, NPO/hospitalized.
items.reference: Biguanides: "Metformin... reduces hepatic glucose production... The major toxicity of metformin, lactic acidosis, is very rare... Metformin should not be used in patients with moderate renal insufficiency (GFR <30 mL/min), any form of acidosis, unstable congestive heart failure, liver disease."

items.title: Why sulfonylureas cause hypoglycemia and weight gain
items.content:
Sulfonylureas (and glinides) bind the ATP-sensitive K+ channel on the beta cell, forcing insulin secretion REGARDLESS of glucose level — so unlike incretins they can drive glucose too low, especially with delayed meals, exercise, alcohol, renal insufficiency, or in the elderly. The extra insulin and improved control also cause weight gain.

Practical points: they work best in recent-onset (<5 yr) type 2 DM with residual beta-cell function; glimepiride/glipizide are preferred over glyburide (less hypoglycemia), especially in the elderly; glinides are taken before each meal for postprandial control. Stop sulfonylureas when adding interacting antibiotics (fluoroquinolones, clarithromycin, TMP-SMX, metronidazole, fluconazole).
keyPoints: Glucose-INDEPENDENT insulin release → hypoglycemia + weight gain.
Prefer glimepiride/glipizide over glyburide (elderly).
Best in recent-onset type 2 with residual insulin secretion.
items.reference: Insulin Secretagogues: "Insulin secretagogues, especially the longer-acting ones, have the potential to cause hypoglycemia... Weight gain, a common side effect... results from the increased insulin levels."

items.title: Why GLP-1 receptor agonists are so advantageous in type 2 DM
items.content:
GLP-1RAs amplify glucose-stimulated insulin secretion, suppress glucagon, and slow gastric emptying. Because the insulin effect is GLUCOSE-DEPENDENT, they do NOT cause hypoglycemia on their own (only when combined with insulin/sulfonylureas). They also promote WEIGHT LOSS and reduce cardiovascular events in type 2 DM with ASCVD (liraglutide, semaglutide, dulaglutide) and slow diabetic kidney disease.

Most are weekly injections (semaglutide, dulaglutide, exenatide-ER); oral semaglutide also exists. Main side effect is nausea (start low). Black-box warning: thyroid C-cell tumors in rodents → contraindicated in medullary thyroid carcinoma/MEN 2. Avoid with significant pancreatitis risk.
keyPoints: Glucose-dependent insulin + glucagon suppression → no intrinsic hypoglycemia.
Weight loss + cardiovascular and renal benefit.
Contraindicated with MTC/MEN 2 (C-cell tumor warning); caution if pancreatitis risk.
items.reference: GLP-1RAs: "Agents in this class do not cause hypoglycemia because of the glucose-dependent nature of incretin-stimulated insulin secretion... promote weight loss... and reduce cardiovascular events... contraindicated in individuals with medullary carcinoma of the thyroid or multiple endocrine neoplasia."

items.title: How SGLT-2 inhibitors work and why they cause euglycemic DKA
items.content:
SGLT-2 inhibitors block glucose reabsorption in the proximal tubule, lowering the renal threshold so glucose is lost in urine — an INSULIN-INDEPENDENT mechanism, giving modest weight loss and (via natriuresis) a 3–6 mmHg fall in systolic BP. They reduce ASCVD events (empagliflozin, canagliflozin), heart-failure hospitalization, and progression of diabetic kidney disease.

But by lowering glucose and increasing glucagon, they raise ketone production; during illness or fasting the glucosuria can mask insulin needs, producing EUGLYCEMIC DKA (DKA with near-normal glucose). Side effects: genital/urinary mycotic infections, volume depletion. Don't start if eGFR <45; don't use if <30; not for type 1 or pancreatogenic (insulin-deficient) DM.
keyPoints: Urinary glucose loss, insulin-independent; weight loss + BP drop.
CV, heart-failure, and renal benefit.
Euglycemic DKA risk; genital mycotic infection; avoid in insulin-deficient DM and low eGFR.
items.reference: SGLT-2 Inhibitors: "Inhibition of SGLT-2 may lead to increased glucagon and... ketones. Euglycemic DKA may occur during illness... These agents should not be prescribed for patients with type 1 DM or pancreatogenic forms of DM."

items.title: Why GLP-1RA or SGLT-2 inhibitor is favored beyond glucose control
items.content:
Older trials (UKPDS, ACCORD, ADVANCE, VADT) showed glycemic control reduces MICROvascular complications, but tight control did not consistently reduce macrovascular events and hypoglycemia harmed high-CV-risk patients. The game-changer: GLP-1RAs and SGLT-2 inhibitors independently reduce cardiovascular events and slow kidney-disease progression.

Therefore, in most patients with type 2 DM, a GLP-1RA or SGLT-2 inhibitor should be used (added to metformin), with the choice driven by whether ASCVD, heart failure, or CKD is present or whether weight loss is a major goal — not by glucose alone.
keyPoints: Tight glucose control mainly reduces microvascular disease; avoid hypoglycemia in high-CV-risk patients.
GLP-1RA / SGLT-2 inhibitor reduce CV events and slow CKD independent of glucose.
Pick the agent by comorbidity: ASCVD, heart failure, CKD, or weight goal.
items.reference: Combination Therapy: "GLP-1RAs and SGLT-2 inhibitors reduce cardiovascular events and slow the progression of renal disease, indicating that a GLP-1RA or an SGLT-2 inhibitor should be used in most individuals with type 2 DM."

items.title: Pioglitazone: mechanism and the safety issues that limit it
items.content:
Thiazolidinediones (pioglitazone, rosiglitazone) bind PPAR-γ, reducing insulin resistance (they take days to act and do not cause hypoglycemia). Pioglitazone raises HDL and lowers triglycerides.

But adverse effects constrain use: weight gain (2–3 kg), fluid retention with peripheral edema and heart failure (contraindicated in NYHA class III/IV), increased fractures in postmenopausal women, worsened diabetic macular edema, and (pioglitazone) a possible increased bladder-cancer risk. They are contraindicated in hepatic insufficiency and CHF, and induce ovulation in PCOS (warn about pregnancy risk).
keyPoints: PPAR-γ agonist → lowers insulin resistance; no hypoglycemia, acts over days.
Fluid retention/heart failure (avoid class III/IV CHF); fractures; macular edema.
Pioglitazone: possible bladder cancer; can trigger ovulation in PCOS.
items.reference: Thiazolidinediones: "Peripheral edema and CHF are more common... contraindicated in patients with hepatic insufficiency or CHF (class III or IV)... An increased risk of fractures... pioglitazone may be associated with an increased risk of bladder cancer."

items.title: How insulin deficiency plus counterregulation produces DKA
items.content:
DKA results from relative/absolute insulin deficiency PLUS excess counterregulatory hormones (glucagon, catecholamines, cortisol, growth hormone). The low insulin:glucagon ratio drives hepatic gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis (hyperglycemia) and shifts the liver toward ketone-body synthesis. Reduced insulin and high catecholamines/GH increase lipolysis, flooding the liver with free fatty acids that are converted to ketoacids → high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis.

Ketonemia (not just hyperglycemia) is the consistent, defining feature. β-hydroxybutyrate is made ~3× faster than acetoacetate, so blood β-hydroxybutyrate is the preferred test (urine nitroprusside detects only acetoacetate/acetone and can mislead).
keyPoints: Low insulin:glucagon → hyperglycemia + hepatic ketogenesis.
Lipolysis supplies FFAs → ketoacids → anion-gap acidosis.
Measure blood β-hydroxybutyrate (nitroprusside misses it).
items.reference: DKA — Pathophysiology: "DKA results from relative or absolute insulin deficiency combined with counterregulatory hormone excess (glucagon, catecholamines, cortisol, and growth hormone)."

items.title: Why potassium can look normal in DKA yet must be watched closely
items.content:
Total-body potassium is markedly depleted in DKA (~3–5 mmol/kg), but the SERUM potassium at presentation is often normal or even high because acidosis and insulin deficiency shift potassium OUT of cells. Treatment reverses this: insulin and correction of acidosis drive potassium INTO cells, and urinary losses continue — so serum potassium can fall sharply.

Two rules follow: (1) if the initial K+ is <3.3 mmol/L, do NOT give insulin until potassium is replaced (insulin would precipitate dangerous hypokalemia/arrhythmia); (2) start potassium replacement once urine output is adequate and serum K+ is normal/low, aiming to keep K+ 4–5 mmol/L.
keyPoints: Total-body K+ depleted; serum K+ may be normal/high initially.
If initial K+ <3.3 → correct potassium BEFORE starting insulin.
Insulin + fluids drop K+ → replace early once urine output is adequate.
items.reference: DKA — Treatment: "If the initial serum potassium is <3.3 mmol/L... do not administer insulin until the potassium is corrected... potassium repletion should commence as soon as adequate urine output and a normal serum potassium are documented."

items.title: Core management of DKA
items.content:
Pillars: (1) FLUIDS — 2–3 L of 0.9% saline (or lactated Ringer's) over 1–3 h, then 0.45% saline; switch to dextrose-containing fluid when glucose reaches ~250 mg/dL to allow continued insulin without hypoglycemia. (2) INSULIN — regular insulin 0.1 U/kg IV bolus then 0.1 U/kg/h infusion (increase if no response by 2–4 h); reduce to 0.02–0.1 U/kg/h once improving. (3) POTASSIUM — replace as above. (4) Find and treat the precipitant (infection, MI, missed insulin).

Key nuances: glucose normalizes faster than ketoacidosis, so continue insulin + dextrose until the anion gap/bicarbonate normalize (≥18). Bicarbonate only if pH <7.0 (no proven benefit). Give long-acting insulin once the patient is eating, overlapping the infusion by 2–4 h. Watch for cerebral edema (children).
keyPoints: Fluids + IV regular insulin 0.1 U/kg/h + potassium; treat the precipitant.
Add dextrose at glucose ~250; continue insulin until acidosis resolves.
Bicarbonate only if pH <7.0; overlap long-acting insulin before stopping the drip.
items.reference: DKA — Treatment: "A bolus of IV (0.1 units/kg) short-acting regular insulin... 0.1 units/kg per hour by continuous IV infusion... Long-acting insulin... should be administered as soon as the patient resumes eating... Bicarbonate replacement has not been shown to improve outcomes."

items.title: Why amylase and abdominal pain can mislead in DKA
items.content:
DKA frequently causes severe abdominal pain (mimicking acute pancreatitis or a ruptured viscus) and a raised serum amylase — but in DKA the amylase is usually of SALIVARY origin and does NOT indicate pancreatitis. So an elevated amylase alone should not trigger a pancreatitis diagnosis; order serum LIPASE if pancreatitis is genuinely suspected.

Likewise, the abdominal pain often resolves with treatment of the DKA itself. Conversely, a normal serum sodium in DKA actually signals a MORE profound free-water deficit (sodium is normally diluted by hyperglycemia: subtract 1.6 mmol/L per 100 mg/dL glucose rise).
keyPoints: Hyperamylasemia in DKA is usually salivary — not pancreatitis; check lipase.
Abdominal pain often resolves as DKA is treated.
A 'normal' sodium in DKA means a larger water deficit (correct Na for glucose).
items.reference: DKA — Laboratory: "in DKA the amylase is usually of salivary origin and thus is not diagnostic of pancreatitis. Serum lipase should be obtained if pancreatitis is suspected... A normal serum sodium in the setting of DKA indicates a more profound water deficit."

items.title: Why HHS has hyperosmolarity but little ketosis
items.content:
Hyperglycemic hyperosmolar state (HHS) typically affects an elderly type 2 patient over days–weeks: profound hyperglycemia (often >1000 mg/dL) drives an osmotic diuresis and severe dehydration with hyperosmolality (>300), but ketosis/acidosis are absent or mild. The reason is that the insulin deficiency is only RELATIVE — enough insulin remains to suppress lipolysis/ketogenesis (and counterregulatory hormones/free fatty acids are lower than in DKA), so ketoacids don't accumulate.

Clinically there is NO Kussmaul breathing, nausea/vomiting, or abdominal pain (unlike DKA); presentation is confusion/lethargy/coma from hyperosmolality, usually precipitated by infection, MI, stroke, or impaired water access. Mortality is higher than DKA (up to 15%).
keyPoints: Relative insulin deficiency → enough to block ketogenesis → minimal acidosis.
Massive hyperglycemia + dehydration + hyperosmolality → altered mental status.
No Kussmaul/abdominal pain; mortality up to 15% (higher than DKA).
items.reference: HHS — Pathophysiology: "The absence of ketosis in HHS is not understood. Presumably, the insulin deficiency is only relative and less severe than in DKA... HHS has a substantially higher mortality rate than DKA (up to 15%)."

items.title: Treating HHS: fluids first, careful osmolality correction
items.content:
HHS and DKA share volume depletion and need insulin, but in HHS the fluid deficit (often 9–10 L) is larger and accumulated over days–weeks. Management: stabilize hemodynamics with 1–3 L of 0.9% saline over 2–3 h; then correct the free-water deficit with hypotonic fluid (0.45% saline, later D5W) over 1–2 days. If serum sodium is >150, use 0.45% saline.

Reverse the hyperosmolar state SLOWLY — too-rapid correction can worsen neurologic function (cerebral edema). Insulin: 0.1 U/kg bolus then 0.1 U/kg/h, add dextrose when glucose reaches 200–250, and continue until the patient eats; discharge on insulin (some can later switch to oral agents).
keyPoints: Larger fluid deficit (9–10 L); saline first, then hypotonic fluids over 1–2 days.
Correct hyperosmolality slowly to avoid neurologic harm.
Insulin 0.1 U/kg/h; add dextrose at glucose 200–250; discharge on insulin.
items.reference: HHS — Treatment: "the rapidity of reversal of the hyperosmolar state must balance the need for free water repletion with the risk that too rapid a reversal may worsen neurologic function... The calculated free water deficit... should be reversed over the next 1–2 days."

items.title: Why very tight glucose control harms hospitalized patients
items.content:
Hyperglycemia predicts poor outcomes in hospital, but the NICE-SUGAR trial showed that VERY strict control (target 81–108 mg/dL) in ICU patients INCREASED mortality and severe hypoglycemia compared with a moderate target (<180). The harm of hypoglycemia outweighs the benefit of near-normoglycemia in acutely ill patients.

ADA inpatient targets therefore are 140–180 mg/dL (most critically/non-critically ill), 110–140 in selected patients, and 80–180 perioperatively. 'Sliding-scale' insulin alone is inadequate — use scheduled basal insulin plus prandial/correction rapid-acting insulin.
keyPoints: NICE-SUGAR: tight control (81–108) → more deaths and hypoglycemia.
Inpatient target 140–180 mg/dL (perioperative 80–180).
Use basal-bolus, not sliding scale alone.
items.reference: Hospital management: "NICE-SUGAR... found an increased mortality rate and a greater number of episodes of severe hypoglycemia with very strict glycemic control... compared to individuals with a more moderate glycemic goal."

items.title: Why oral diabetes agents are stopped in hospital
items.content:
Hospitalized patients have rapidly changing insulin requirements and intake, so oral agents are unreliable and sometimes dangerous: sulfonylureas cause hypoglycemia if the patient becomes NPO; metformin risks lactic acidosis with contrast, declining renal function, or unstable CHF; SGLT-2 inhibitors can cause euglycemic DKA when fasting/ill (stop them up to a week before planned admission).

Insulin is the inpatient drug of choice — an IV infusion (regular insulin, short half-life) in the ICU/unstable patients, or scheduled SC basal-bolus on the wards. When stopping an IV insulin drip, give long-acting SC insulin 2–4 h before to avoid a gap of insulin deficiency.
keyPoints: Stop orals on admission (sulfonylurea hypoglycemia, metformin lactic acidosis, SGLT-2 euglycemic DKA).
Insulin is the inpatient drug of choice (IV infusion in ICU; basal-bolus on wards).
Overlap long-acting insulin 2–4 h before stopping an IV insulin drip.
items.reference: Hospital management: "Oral glucose-lowering agents should be discontinued upon admission... these oral agents may be dangerous if the patient is fasting (e.g., hypoglycemia with sulfonylureas, euglycemic DKA with SGLT-2 inhibitors)... lactic acidosis with metformin."

items.title: Pramlintide: the amylin analogue for insulin-treated patients
items.content:
Pramlintide, an amylin analogue, is given before meals in insulin-treated type 1 or type 2 DM. It slows gastric emptying and suppresses glucagon (it does NOT change insulin levels), modestly lowering HbA1c and blunting post-meal glucose excursions.

Key safety points: because it suppresses glucagon, it can impair recovery from hypoglycemia, so it should NOT be used in patients with hypoglycemia unawareness; the prandial rapid-acting insulin must be reduced initially to avoid hypoglycemia; it slows GI motility (don't combine with other gut-slowing drugs and beware altered absorption of other medications). Main side effects: nausea/vomiting.
keyPoints: Slows gastric emptying + suppresses glucagon; cuts postprandial glucose.
Reduce mealtime insulin when starting; avoid in hypoglycemia unawareness.
Nausea is the main side effect.
items.reference: Pramlintide: "Pramlintide injected just before a meal slows gastric emptying and suppresses glucagon but does not alter insulin levels... it may worsen hypoglycemia recovery and should not be used in patients with hypoglycemia unawareness."

items.title: Why type 1 patients can swing either way with exercise
items.content:
Exercise lowers glucose and raises insulin sensitivity, but type 1 patients lack normal glucoregulation (normally insulin falls and glucagon rises during exercise). Outcome depends on the circulating insulin level: if insulin is TOO LOW, catecholamines and lactate drive hepatic glucose output up and promote ketosis (hyperglycemia/DKA); if insulin is EXCESSIVE, hepatic glucose output is suppressed and muscle uptake rises → hypoglycemia.

Practical rules: monitor glucose before/during/after; delay exercise if glucose >250 with ketones; eat carbohydrate if glucose <90; reduce insulin doses around exercise and inject into a non-exercising site. ADA advises 150 min/week of aerobic activity (no gap >2 days). Untreated proliferative retinopathy is a relative contraindication to vigorous exercise (vitreous hemorrhage/retinal detachment risk).
keyPoints: Too little insulin → hyperglycemia/ketosis; too much → hypoglycemia.
Hold exercise if glucose >250 with ketones; carbs if <90.
Proliferative retinopathy = relative contraindication to vigorous exercise.
items.reference: Physical Activity: "Individuals with type 1 DM are prone to either hyperglycemia or hypoglycemia during exercise... delay exercise if blood glucose is >14 mmol/L (250 mg/dL) and ketones are present... Untreated proliferative retinopathy is a relative contraindication to vigorous exercise."

items.title: Why preconception glucose control is critical in diabetes
items.content:
Glucose is a teratogen and freely crosses the placenta, but insulin does not — so maternal hyperglycemia raises fetal glucose, stimulating fetal insulin secretion. Fetal hyperinsulinism drives macrosomia and neonatal hypoglycemia. The most crucial window is soon after fertilization, during organogenesis.

Therefore, women with pre-existing diabetes need preconception near-normalization of HbA1c (<6.5%), because uncontrolled diabetes at conception raises fetal malformation risk 4–10 fold. Oral agents are not approved in pregnancy (metformin/glyburide have been studied); insulin (often with CGM/AID) is standard. GDM is screened at 24–28 weeks.
keyPoints: Glucose crosses the placenta (insulin doesn't) → fetal hyperinsulinism → macrosomia.
Preconception HbA1c <6.5%; uncontrolled DM raises malformations 4–10×.
Insulin is standard in pregnancy; screen GDM at 24–28 weeks.
items.reference: Reproductive Issues: "The risk of fetal malformations is increased 4–10 times in individuals with uncontrolled DM at the time of conception... Glucose, which at high levels is a teratogen... readily crosses the placenta, but insulin does not."

items.title: Why hypoglycemia on an alpha-glucosidase inhibitor needs pure glucose
items.content:
Alpha-glucosidase inhibitors (acarbose, miglitol) are taken before meals and inhibit the intestinal enzyme that cleaves oligosaccharides into absorbable simple sugars, blunting the postprandial glucose rise. They are less potent at lowering HbA1c but uniquely target postprandial glucose; main side effects are GI (flatulence, diarrhea, distention) from undigested carbohydrate reaching the colon.

Key clinical pearl: if hypoglycemia occurs (e.g., from a co-administered sulfonylurea/insulin), the patient must take pure GLUCOSE — not complex carbohydrates or table sugar — because the drug slows the breakdown/absorption of complex carbohydrates and sucrose.
keyPoints: Delays carbohydrate absorption → lowers postprandial glucose; GI side effects.
Treat hypoglycemia with glucose (not sucrose/complex carbs).
Less potent on HbA1c; can raise sulfonylurea levels.
items.reference: α-Glucosidase Inhibitors: "If hypoglycemia from other diabetes treatments occurs while taking these agents, the patient should consume glucose because the degradation and absorption of complex carbohydrates will be slowed."

items.title: DPP-4 inhibitors: modest, weight-neutral, watch for specific reactions
items.content:
DPP-4 inhibitors (sitagliptin, etc.) block the enzyme that degrades native GLP-1 and GIP, enhancing the incretin effect. They promote insulin secretion WITHOUT hypoglycemia or weight gain and act preferentially on postprandial glucose, but the level of GLP-1 action is lower than with GLP-1RAs (so they are less potent and don't drive weight loss).

Use alone or with other oral agents; reduce the dose in renal insufficiency. Watch for hypersensitivity reactions (rash, angioedema, anaphylaxis, Stevens-Johnson syndrome) and severe joint pain; there is a small possible pancreatitis signal (less than GLP-1RAs) — avoid with significant pancreatitis risk.
keyPoints: Enhance incretins; no hypoglycemia/weight gain; postprandial effect.
Less potent than GLP-1RAs; reduce dose in renal impairment.
Watch hypersensitivity (incl. SJS/angioedema), joint pain, pancreatitis risk.
items.reference: DPP-4 inhibitors: "DPP-4 inhibitors promote insulin secretion in the absence of hypoglycemia or weight gain... Allergy, including rash, hypersensitivity reactions (including anaphylaxis, angioedema, and Stevens-Johnson syndrome), and severe joint pain have been reported."

items.title: Why basal insulin is started at bedtime in type 2 DM
items.content:
Type 2 DM is progressive, and many patients eventually need insulin. Because fasting hyperglycemia from increased hepatic glucose output is a prominent feature, the first insulin step is a single long-acting (basal) dose — 0.1–0.4 U/kg (or a fixed 10–15 units) given in the EVENING/at bedtime, which is more effective than morning dosing in trials. Glargine at bedtime causes less nocturnal hypoglycemia than NPH.

Basal insulin combines well with oral agents (and GLP-1RAs, which limit weight gain). As beta-cell function declines, prandial rapid-acting insulin is added (basal-bolus), and total doses can become large (1–2 U/kg/day) with insulin resistance/weight gain.
keyPoints: Start basal insulin at bedtime — targets fasting (hepatic) hyperglycemia.
Glargine bedtime < NPH for nocturnal hypoglycemia.
Add prandial insulin as the disease progresses; combine with GLP-1RA to limit weight gain.
items.reference: Insulin Therapy in Type 2 DM: "Because fasting hyperglycemia and increased hepatic glucose production are prominent features of type 2 DM, bedtime insulin is more effective in clinical trials than a single dose of morning insulin."

items.title: Why euglycemic DKA is easy to miss
items.content:
SGLT-2-inhibitor-associated DKA can occur with a NORMAL or only mildly elevated glucose, because the ongoing glucosuria keeps blood glucose down even as ketoacidosis develops during illness/fasting. The absence of marked hyperglycemia means the usual trigger for testing is missing — so clinicians must check ketones/acid-base in an unwell patient on an SGLT-2 inhibitor even if glucose looks fine.

Management differs from classic DKA in two ways: start dextrose AND insulin together when 0.9% saline is begun (because glucose is already low), and base RESOLUTION on bicarbonate/anion-gap correction, not on glucose.
keyPoints: Glucosuria masks hyperglycemia → DKA at near-normal glucose.
Test ketones/acid-base in any unwell SGLT-2 user regardless of glucose.
Treat with dextrose + insulin together; judge resolution by bicarbonate, not glucose.
items.reference: Severe Hyperglycemia/DKA: "DKA can also occur in the setting of treatment of type 2 DM with an SGLT-2 inhibitor. Often the blood glucose is normal or just mildly elevated because of the glucosuria... Resolution of euglycemic DKA should be based on bicarbonate, not glucose, correction."

items.title: Inhaled insulin: faster onset, but lung precautions
items.content:
Inhaled insulin provides mealtime coverage with a more rapid onset than subcutaneous injection. However, it can cause bronchospasm and cough, so FEV1 must be measured before starting and monitored periodically, and it should NOT be used by people with lung disease or who smoke.

It is a prandial option only (basal insulin is still needed). This is a niche tool — most patients use injected rapid-acting analogues or pumps.
keyPoints: Rapid prandial onset; basal insulin still required.
Check FEV1 before/during use.
Avoid in lung disease and smokers (bronchospasm/cough).
items.reference: Insulin Preparations: "Inhaled insulin can cause bronchospasm and cough and should not be used by individuals with lung disease or those who smoke."

items.title: Why CGM helps most in those at risk of hypoglycemia
items.content:
CGM measures INTERSTITIAL glucose (in equilibrium with blood but lagging during rapid changes/exercise) every 5 minutes and reports time-in-range, time below/above range, and a glucose management indicator that correlates with HbA1c. Real-time trend arrows let patients pre-empt predicted highs and lows.

It is especially valuable in type 1 DM with HYPOGLYCEMIA UNAWARENESS, where it reduces serious (especially nocturnal) hypoglycemia, and when paired with a pump it enables predictive low-glucose suspension or closed-loop control. Patients on CGM still need a fingerstick meter for malfunctions/calibration; substances such as acetaminophen, ascorbic acid, and hydroxyurea can interfere with CGM readings.
keyPoints: Interstitial glucose lags blood during rapid change/exercise.
Biggest benefit: type 1 with hypoglycemia unawareness (fewer nocturnal lows).
Keep a fingerstick backup; beware interfering substances (acetaminophen, etc.).
items.reference: Short-Term Glycemic Control: "CGM in type 1 DM, especially in those with hypoglycemia unawareness, can decrease the frequency of serious hypoglycemia (especially nocturnal hypoglycemia)."

items.title: Why rapid glucose lowering early can restore beta-cell function
items.content:
Any therapy that improves glycemic control reduces 'glucose toxicity' to beta cells and may improve endogenous insulin secretion. This is the rationale for using insulin as INITIAL therapy in newly diagnosed type 2 DM with severe hyperglycemia (e.g., FPG >250–300 mg/dL) or catabolic symptoms: rapid control relieves glucose toxicity, can improve the islet's own secretion, and may later allow oral agents to work — sometimes the insulin can then be discontinued.

Similarly, very-low-calorie diets or metabolic surgery in recent-onset type 2 DM can dramatically improve or even resolve the disease.
keyPoints: High glucose is toxic to beta cells (reversible) — lower it promptly.
Initial insulin for severe hyperglycemia can restore endogenous secretion.
Early VLCD or metabolic surgery can resolve recent-onset type 2 DM.
items.reference: Type 2 DM: "Any therapy that improves glycemic control reduces 'glucose toxicity' to beta cells and may improve endogenous insulin secretion... more rapid glycemic control will reduce 'glucose toxicity' to the islet cells, improve endogenous insulin secretion."

items.question: Which oral agent is recommended as initial pharmacologic therapy for most patients with newly diagnosed type 2 diabetes?
items.explanation: Metformin is first-line because of its efficacy, favorable side-effect profile, mild weight loss, lack of hypoglycemia, and low cost. Sulfonylureas cause hypoglycemia/weight gain; thiazolidinediones cause fluid retention; alpha-glucosidase inhibitors are less potent.
items.reference: Choice of Initial Agent: "Treatment algorithms... suggest metformin as initial therapy because of its efficacy, known side effect profile, and low cost."

items.question: In which situation should metformin be withheld?
options: A patient with HbA1c 8.5% and normal renal function
A patient about to receive intravenous iodinated contrast
A patient with a fasting glucose of 150 mg/dL
items.explanation: Metformin should be withheld before iodinated contrast (and in GFR <30, acidosis, unstable CHF, liver disease, hypoxemia, or when NPO/hospitalized) because of the rare risk of lactic acidosis; insulin is used until it can be restarted.
items.reference: Biguanides: "Metformin should be discontinued in hospitalized patients, in patients who can take nothing orally, and in those receiving radiographic contrast material."

items.question: A GLP-1 receptor agonist is contraindicated in a patient with which condition?
items.explanation: GLP-1RAs carry a black-box warning for thyroid C-cell tumors (seen in rodents) and are contraindicated in medullary thyroid carcinoma and MEN 2. They are actually favored in ASCVD, obesity, and CKD because of cardiovascular/renal/weight benefits.
items.reference: GLP-1RAs: "contraindicated in individuals with medullary carcinoma of the thyroid or multiple endocrine neoplasia."

items.question: Why do GLP-1 receptor agonists generally NOT cause hypoglycemia when used alone?
options: Their stimulation of insulin secretion is glucose-dependent (and they suppress glucagon)
items.explanation: GLP-1RAs augment insulin secretion only when glucose is elevated (glucose-dependent) and suppress glucagon, so they don't drive glucose below normal on their own. Hypoglycemia risk arises only when combined with insulin or sulfonylureas.
items.reference: GLP-1RAs: "Agents in this class do not cause hypoglycemia because of the glucose-dependent nature of incretin-stimulated insulin secretion."

items.question: A type 2 diabetic on an SGLT-2 inhibitor presents unwell with vomiting; glucose is 180 mg/dL but there is a high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis with ketonemia. What is the diagnosis?
items.explanation: SGLT-2 inhibitors can cause euglycemic DKA — ketoacidosis with near-normal glucose, because glucosuria masks hyperglycemia. Test ketones/acid-base in any unwell SGLT-2 user regardless of glucose. Treat with dextrose plus insulin, judging resolution by bicarbonate, not glucose.
items.reference: SGLT-2 Inhibitors: "Euglycemic DKA may occur during illness or when ongoing glucosuria masks stress-induced requirements for insulin."

items.question: An SGLT-2 inhibitor should NOT be initiated in which patient?
options: A type 2 diabetic with heart failure
A type 2 diabetic with eGFR 40 mL/min/1.73 m² (stage 3b CKD)
A type 2 diabetic seeking weight loss
items.explanation: SGLT-2 inhibitors should not be initiated in stage 3b CKD (eGFR <45) and should not be used in stage 4 (eGFR <30). They are actually beneficial in heart failure, ASCVD, and for weight loss. They are also contraindicated in type 1/insulin-deficient DM (DKA risk).
items.reference: SGLT-2 Inhibitors: "should not be initiated in patients with stage 3b chronic kidney disease (eGFR <45 mL/min per 1.73 m²) and should not be used in stage 4 CKD (eGFR <30)."

items.question: Beyond glucose lowering, which two drug classes are now recommended for most patients with type 2 diabetes because they reduce cardiovascular events and slow kidney disease?
options: GLP-1 receptor agonists and SGLT-2 inhibitors
items.explanation: GLP-1RAs and SGLT-2 inhibitors independently reduce cardiovascular events and slow renal disease progression, so one should be used in most patients with type 2 DM, with the specific choice guided by ASCVD, heart failure, CKD, or weight-loss goals.
items.reference: Combination Therapy: "GLP-1RAs and SGLT-2 inhibitors reduce cardiovascular events and slow the progression of renal disease, indicating that a GLP-1RA or an SGLT-2 inhibitor should be used in most individuals with type 2 DM."

items.question: A thiazolidinedione (e.g., pioglitazone) is contraindicated in a patient with which condition?
options: NYHA class III–IV congestive heart failure
items.explanation: Thiazolidinediones cause fluid retention and can precipitate or worsen heart failure, so they are contraindicated in class III/IV CHF (and hepatic insufficiency). Other concerns: weight gain, fractures in postmenopausal women, macular edema, and possible bladder cancer with pioglitazone.
items.reference: Thiazolidinediones: "These agents are contraindicated in patients with hepatic insufficiency or CHF (class III or IV)."

items.question: Which sulfonylurea property explains its risk of hypoglycemia?
options: It only works when glucose is high
It stimulates insulin secretion independent of the glucose level via the ATP-sensitive K+ channel
items.explanation: Sulfonylureas close the beta-cell ATP-sensitive K+ channel and force insulin secretion regardless of glucose level, so they can drive glucose too low — especially with missed meals, exercise, alcohol, renal insufficiency, or in the elderly. They also cause weight gain.
items.reference: Insulin Secretagogues: "Insulin secretagogues stimulate insulin secretion by interacting with the ATP-sensitive potassium channel on the beta cell... have the potential to cause hypoglycemia."

items.question: How far before a meal should a rapid-acting insulin analogue (lispro, aspart, glulisine) typically be injected?
items.explanation: Rapid-acting analogues are injected just before the meal (<10 min) because their fast onset matches the post-meal glucose rise. Regular insulin, by contrast, must be given 30–45 minutes before the meal.
items.reference: Insulin Regimens: "Rapid-acting insulin analogues should be injected just before (<10 min) and regular insulin 30–45 min prior to a meal."

items.question: Why can a patient on an insulin pump (CSII) develop DKA quickly if the infusion set occludes?
options: Pumps deliver long-acting insulin that accumulates
Pumps use only rapid-acting insulin with a short half-life, so interruption rapidly causes insulin deficiency
items.explanation: Pumps use only rapid-acting analogues (short half-life) and provide no long-acting depot, so an occlusion or disconnection leads to insulin deficiency within hours and can precipitate DKA. Pump users need a backup injection plan.
items.reference: Insulin pumps: "the short half-life of these insulins quickly leads to insulin deficiency if the delivery system is interrupted."

items.question: A patient in DKA has an initial serum potassium of 3.0 mmol/L. What is the correct next step?
options: Replace potassium first and withhold insulin until potassium is corrected
items.explanation: If the initial potassium is <3.3 mmol/L, insulin must be withheld until potassium is replaced, because insulin drives potassium into cells and would precipitate dangerous hypokalemia and arrhythmia. Total-body potassium is depleted even when serum potassium initially looks normal/high.
items.reference: DKA — Treatment: "If the initial serum potassium is <3.3 mmol/L (3.3 meq/L), do not administer insulin until the potassium is corrected."

items.question: Which test most accurately reflects the ketone level in DKA?
items.explanation: β-hydroxybutyrate is produced ~3× faster than acetoacetate in DKA, but the urine nitroprusside reagent detects only acetoacetate/acetone, so it underestimates ketosis. Blood/serum β-hydroxybutyrate is the preferred, more accurate measure.
items.reference: DKA — Laboratory: "Serum or plasma assays for β-hydroxybutyrate are preferred because they more accurately reflect the true ketone body level."

items.question: During DKA treatment, when should dextrose be added to the intravenous fluids?
options: When the blood glucose falls to about 250 mg/dL (so insulin can continue to clear ketoacidosis without causing hypoglycemia)
Only after the patient is eating
items.explanation: Dextrose is added when glucose reaches ~250 mg/dL so the insulin infusion can be continued to resolve the ketoacidosis (which clears more slowly than hyperglycemia) without causing hypoglycemia. Insulin is continued until the anion gap/bicarbonate normalize.
items.reference: DKA — Treatment (Table 416-9): "change to 5–10% glucose and 0.45% saline... when blood glucose reaches 250 mg/dL (13.9 mmol/L)."

items.question: When is intravenous sodium bicarbonate indicated in DKA?
options: When the anion gap is elevated
items.explanation: Bicarbonate has not been shown to improve outcomes and is reserved for severe acidosis (arterial pH <7.0), given until the pH exceeds 7.0. Routine bicarbonate is not recommended.
items.reference: DKA — Treatment: "Bicarbonate replacement has not been shown to improve outcomes. However, in the presence of severe acidosis (arterial pH <7.0), sodium bicarbonate... may be administered."

items.question: A patient in DKA has a markedly elevated serum amylase and severe abdominal pain. How should this be interpreted?
options: The amylase is usually salivary in DKA and is not diagnostic of pancreatitis; check serum lipase if pancreatitis is suspected
items.explanation: In DKA the amylase is usually of salivary origin and does not indicate pancreatitis; serum lipase should be obtained if pancreatitis is genuinely suspected. Abdominal pain in DKA often resolves with treatment.
items.reference: DKA — Laboratory: "in DKA the amylase is usually of salivary origin and thus is not diagnostic of pancreatitis. Serum lipase should be obtained if pancreatitis is suspected."

items.question: Which serious nonmetabolic complication of DKA treatment occurs most often in children as the DKA is resolving?
items.explanation: Cerebral edema is an extremely serious complication of DKA, occurring most frequently in children, typically as the DKA is resolving. A mild hyperchloremic acidosis commonly follows treatment but is benign and self-limited.
items.reference: DKA — Treatment: "The major nonmetabolic complication of DKA therapy is cerebral edema, which most often develops in children as DKA is resolving."

items.question: Compared with DKA, hyperglycemic hyperosmolar state (HHS) is characterized by which feature?
options: Much higher glucose and osmolality with absent or only mild ketosis/acidosis
Occurrence mainly in young type 1 patients
items.explanation: HHS features marked hyperglycemia (often >1000 mg/dL) and hyperosmolality with profound dehydration but little or no ketosis/acidosis, because residual insulin suppresses ketogenesis. It occurs mainly in elderly type 2 patients and has a higher mortality (up to 15%) than DKA.
items.reference: HHS: "the hyperglycemia is usually greater, leading to hyperosmolarity and marked dehydration but without ketosis or acidosis... HHS has a substantially higher mortality rate than DKA (up to 15%)."

items.question: Why must the hyperosmolar state in HHS be corrected gradually?
options: Because too-rapid reversal of hyperosmolality may worsen neurologic function (cerebral edema)
items.explanation: The fluid/osmolar deficit in HHS accumulated over days–weeks (often 9–10 L); reversing it too quickly can worsen neurologic function. Free-water repletion is therefore spread over 1–2 days, balanced against the need to restore volume.
items.reference: HHS — Treatment: "the rapidity of reversal of the hyperosmolar state must balance the need for free water repletion with the risk that too rapid a reversal may worsen neurologic function."

items.question: Based on NICE-SUGAR and ADA guidance, what is the appropriate glycemic target for most hospitalized patients?
items.explanation: Very tight control (81–108) increased mortality and hypoglycemia in NICE-SUGAR. The ADA recommends 140–180 mg/dL for most critically and non-critically ill patients (110–140 in selected patients; 80–180 perioperatively).
items.reference: Hospital management: "in critically or non-critically ill patients, glucose of 7.8–10.0 mmol/L or 140–180 mg/dL."

items.question: Which approach to inpatient insulin is considered INADEQUATE as the sole regimen?
options: Scheduled basal insulin plus prandial and correction insulin
'Sliding-scale' rapid-acting insulin alone (insulin given only when glucose is high)
Continuous IV insulin infusion in the ICU
items.explanation: 'Sliding-scale' insulin alone, which gives insulin only reactively when glucose is elevated, is inadequate for inpatient management. Scheduled basal insulin plus prandial/correction dosing (or an IV infusion in the ICU) is preferred.
items.reference: Hospital management: "'Sliding scale,' with short-acting or rapid-acting insulin alone, where no insulin is given unless the blood glucose is elevated, is inadequate for inpatient glucose management."

items.question: When discontinuing an intravenous insulin infusion in a patient who will transition to subcutaneous insulin, what must be done?
options: Stop the infusion abruptly with no further insulin
Administer subcutaneous long-acting insulin 2–4 hours before stopping the infusion
Give a large IV insulin bolus
items.explanation: Because IV regular insulin has a very short half-life, long-acting subcutaneous insulin must be given 2–4 hours before stopping the infusion to avoid a gap of insulin deficiency (which could precipitate hyperglycemia/DKA, especially in type 1 DM).
items.reference: Hospital management: "it is necessary to administer long-acting insulin prior to discontinuation of the insulin infusion (2–4 h before the infusion is stopped) to avoid a period of insulin deficiency."

items.question: A woman with type 1 diabetes is planning pregnancy. What preconception HbA1c goal reduces the risk of fetal malformations?
items.explanation: Uncontrolled diabetes at conception increases fetal malformation risk 4–10 fold, and the most crucial control period is soon after fertilization (organogenesis). Near-normalization of HbA1c (<6.5%) before conception is essential.
items.reference: Reproductive Issues: "Intensive insulin therapy and near-normalization of the HbA1c (<6.5%) are essential for individuals with existing DM who are planning pregnancy."

items.question: Why does maternal hyperglycemia cause fetal macrosomia?
options: Maternal insulin crosses the placenta and promotes fetal growth
Glucose crosses the placenta and stimulates fetal insulin secretion, whose anabolic effects cause macrosomia
items.explanation: Glucose crosses the placenta (insulin does not), so maternal hyperglycemia raises fetal glucose and stimulates fetal insulin secretion; insulin's anabolic/growth effects produce macrosomia (and neonatal hypoglycemia after delivery).
items.reference: Reproductive Issues: "hyperglycemia from the maternal circulation may stimulate insulin secretion in the fetus. The anabolic and growth effects of insulin may result in macrosomia."

items.question: A patient on acarbose plus a sulfonylurea develops hypoglycemia. What should be used to treat it?
items.explanation: Alpha-glucosidase inhibitors slow the breakdown and absorption of complex carbohydrates and sucrose, so hypoglycemia must be treated with pure glucose, which is absorbed directly.
items.reference: α-Glucosidase Inhibitors: "the patient should consume glucose because the degradation and absorption of complex carbohydrates will be slowed."

items.question: In which patient should pramlintide be avoided?
options: An insulin-treated type 1 patient with good hypoglycemia awareness
A patient with high postprandial glucose
items.explanation: Pramlintide suppresses glucagon and can worsen recovery from hypoglycemia, so it should not be used in patients with hypoglycemia unawareness. When started, the mealtime rapid-acting insulin should be reduced to avoid hypoglycemia.
items.reference: Pramlintide: "it may worsen hypoglycemia recovery and should not be used in patients with hypoglycemia unawareness."

items.question: When starting insulin in type 2 diabetes, why is a single long-acting (basal) dose typically given at bedtime?
options: Because fasting hyperglycemia from increased hepatic glucose production is prominent, and bedtime basal insulin is more effective than morning dosing
Because basal insulin acts only in the daytime
items.explanation: Fasting hyperglycemia from increased overnight hepatic glucose output is a key feature of type 2 DM, so bedtime basal insulin (more effective than a morning dose in trials) targets it; glargine at bedtime causes less nocturnal hypoglycemia than NPH.
items.reference: Insulin Therapy in Type 2 DM: "Because fasting hyperglycemia and increased hepatic glucose production are prominent features of type 2 DM, bedtime insulin is more effective in clinical trials than a single dose of morning insulin."

items.question: A type 1 diabetic wants to exercise. The glucose is 270 mg/dL and urine ketones are positive. What is the appropriate advice?
options: Delay exercise (high glucose with ketones); exercising could worsen hyperglycemia and ketosis
items.explanation: If glucose is >250 mg/dL with ketones present, exercise should be delayed: low insulin levels mean exercise-induced catecholamines can further raise glucose and promote ketosis/DKA. (If glucose is <90 mg/dL, carbohydrate should be taken before exercise.)
items.reference: Physical Activity: "delay exercise if blood glucose is >14 mmol/L (250 mg/dL) and ketones are present."

items.question: A patient with type 1 diabetes is NPO before surgery. Which approach is correct?
options: Withhold all insulin until the patient can eat
Continue insulin (basal/IV infusion); rapid-acting insulin alone is insufficient, as omitting basal insulin can precipitate DKA
Give rapid-acting insulin only when glucose is high
items.explanation: Patients with type 1 DM always need continuous/basal insulin even when fasting; rapid-acting insulin alone is insufficient, and omitting basal insulin (e.g., during a prolonged procedure) can precipitate DKA. An IV insulin infusion is preferred for prolonged procedures/serious illness.
items.reference: Hospital management: "Individuals with type 1 DM who are undergoing general anesthesia and surgery or who are seriously ill should receive continuous insulin... Rapid-acting insulin alone is insufficient."

items.question: A newly diagnosed type 2 diabetic presents with marked hyperglycemia (FPG 290 mg/dL) and weight loss. What is a reasonable initial therapy?
options: Insulin, to rapidly control hyperglycemia and relieve glucose toxicity (it may later be discontinued)
items.explanation: Insulin can be used as initial therapy for severe hyperglycemia (FPG ~250–300 mg/dL) or symptomatic/catabolic patients; rapid control relieves glucose toxicity, may improve endogenous insulin secretion, and can sometimes be discontinued afterward. Low-potency oral agents are unlikely to achieve control at this level.
items.reference: Choice of Initial Agent: "Insulin can be used as initial therapy in individuals with severe hyperglycemia (FPG <13.9–16.7 mmol/L [250–300 mg/dL]) or in those who are symptomatic from the hyperglycemia."

items.question: In which patient is the HbA1c likely to be UNRELIABLE for assessing glycemic control?
options: A patient with stable type 2 diabetes
A patient with a hemoglobinopathy or on hemodialysis with erythropoietin
items.explanation: Conditions that alter red-cell lifespan or hemoglobin — hemoglobinopathies, anemias, reticulocytosis, transfusion, uremia/hemodialysis, EPO therapy, G6PD variants — distort HbA1c. CGM/glucose or fructosamine/glycated albumin should be used instead.
items.reference: Long-Term Glycemic Control: "Clinical conditions leading to abnormal red blood cell parameters such as hemoglobinopathies, anemias, reticulocytosis, transfusions, uremia... hemodialysis, erythropoietin therapy... may alter the HbA1c result."

items.question: Tirzepatide, used in type 2 diabetes, has what mechanism?
options: Dual agonism at both the GIP receptor and the GLP-1 receptor
items.explanation: Tirzepatide is a once-weekly injectable that is a dual agonist at the GIP receptor and the GLP-1 receptor, producing greater weight loss than a GLP-1RA alone.
items.reference: GLP-1RAs: "Tirzepatide, a once-weekly subcutaneous injectable peptide engineered to have dual agonism at both the glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide receptor (GIPR) and the GLP-1R, promotes greater weight loss than a GLP-1RA alone."

items.statement: Metformin commonly causes hypoglycemia when used as monotherapy.
items.explanation: False. Metformin does not directly cause hypoglycemia; it reduces hepatic glucose production. Hypoglycemia is a risk with insulin and sulfonylureas, not metformin monotherapy.
items.reference: Glucose-Lowering Agents: "biguanides, α-glucosidase inhibitors, GLP-1RAs, DPP-4 inhibitors, thiazolidinediones, and SGLT-2 inhibitors do not directly cause hypoglycemia."

items.statement: SGLT-2 inhibitors are appropriate first-line agents for type 1 diabetes.
items.explanation: False. SGLT-2 inhibitors increase the risk of (euglycemic) DKA and should not be used in type 1 DM or other insulin-deficient (pancreatogenic) forms of diabetes.
items.reference: SGLT-2 Inhibitors: "These agents should not be prescribed for patients with type 1 DM or pancreatogenic forms of DM associated with insulin deficiency."

items.statement: GLP-1 receptor agonists promote weight loss and reduce cardiovascular events in type 2 diabetes with ASCVD.
items.explanation: True. GLP-1RAs promote weight loss and reduce cardiovascular events in patients with type 2 DM and established ASCVD (and slow diabetic kidney disease), which is why they are favored.
items.reference: GLP-1RAs: "These agents promote weight loss... and reduce cardiovascular events in those with type 2 DM and ASCVD."

items.statement: Ketonemia is the consistent finding that distinguishes DKA from simple hyperglycemia.
items.explanation: True. The degree of acidosis and hyperglycemia do not always correlate, but ketonemia is consistently present in DKA and distinguishes it from simple hyperglycemia.
items.reference: DKA — Laboratory: "Ketonemia is a consistent finding in DKA and distinguishes it from simple hyperglycemia."

items.statement: A normal or high serum potassium at presentation in DKA means total-body potassium is adequate.
items.explanation: False. Total-body potassium is markedly depleted in DKA even when serum potassium is normal or high (acidosis and insulin deficiency shift potassium out of cells). Potassium falls sharply with treatment and must be replaced.
items.reference: DKA — Treatment: "Potassium stores are depleted in DKA (estimated deficit 3–5 mmol/kg)... the serum potassium at presentation may be mildly elevated."

items.statement: Routine sodium bicarbonate improves outcomes in DKA and should be given to all patients.
items.explanation: False. Bicarbonate has not been shown to improve outcomes and is reserved for severe acidosis (arterial pH <7.0). Routine use is not recommended.
items.reference: DKA — Treatment: "Bicarbonate replacement has not been shown to improve outcomes."

items.statement: Kussmaul respirations and prominent ketoacidosis are characteristic of hyperglycemic hyperosmolar state.
items.explanation: False. HHS notably LACKS Kussmaul respirations, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain, and significant ketoacidosis; these are features of DKA. HHS is dominated by hyperosmolality and dehydration with altered mental status.
items.reference: HHS — Clinical: "Notably absent are symptoms of nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain and the Kussmaul respirations characteristic of DKA."

items.statement: Very strict glycemic control (81–108 mg/dL) improves survival in critically ill ICU patients.
items.explanation: False. The NICE-SUGAR trial found that very strict control increased mortality and severe hypoglycemia compared with a moderate target (<180 mg/dL). A target of 140–180 mg/dL is recommended for most hospitalized patients.
items.reference: Hospital management: "NICE-SUGAR... found an increased mortality rate and a greater number of episodes of severe hypoglycemia with very strict glycemic control."

items.statement: Oral glucose-lowering agents should be continued during acute hospitalization to maintain control.
items.explanation: False. Oral agents are discontinued on admission because they are unreliable and can be dangerous when intake/insulin requirements change rapidly (sulfonylurea hypoglycemia, metformin lactic acidosis, SGLT-2 euglycemic DKA). Insulin is used instead.
items.reference: Hospital management: "Oral glucose-lowering agents should be discontinued upon admission."

items.statement: Insulin crosses the placenta and directly controls fetal glucose.
items.explanation: False. Glucose crosses the placenta but insulin does not. Maternal hyperglycemia raises fetal glucose, stimulating fetal insulin secretion (causing macrosomia); this is why maternal glucose control is critical.
items.reference: Reproductive Issues: "Glucose... readily crosses the placenta, but insulin does not."

items.statement: Fixed premixed (combination) insulin formulations are appropriate for managing type 1 diabetes.
items.explanation: False. Premixed combinations are convenient but do not allow independent adjustment of short- and long-acting components, so they are not appropriate for type 1 DM, which requires flexible basal-bolus dosing.
items.reference: Insulin Preparations: "combination insulin formulations do not allow independent adjustment of short-acting and long-acting activity and are not appropriate in type 1 DM management."

items.statement: Insulin glargine at bedtime is associated with less nocturnal hypoglycemia than NPH insulin.
items.explanation: True. Glargine has a later onset, ~24-h duration, and a less pronounced peak than NPH, and produces less nocturnal hypoglycemia; degludec offers even fewer nocturnal/severe hypoglycemic events.
items.reference: Insulin Preparations: "A lower incidence of hypoglycemia, especially at night, has been reported with insulin glargine when compared to NPH insulin."

items.statement: Untreated proliferative diabetic retinopathy is a relative contraindication to vigorous exercise.
items.explanation: True. Vigorous exercise may precipitate vitreous hemorrhage or retinal detachment in untreated proliferative retinopathy, so it is a relative contraindication.
items.reference: Physical Activity: "Untreated proliferative retinopathy is a relative contraindication to vigorous exercise because this may lead to vitreous hemorrhage or retinal detachment."

items.statement: Inhaled insulin is appropriate for patients who smoke or have chronic lung disease.
items.explanation: False. Inhaled insulin can cause bronchospasm and cough and should not be used in people with lung disease or who smoke; FEV1 should be checked before and during use.
items.reference: Insulin Preparations: "Inhaled insulin can cause bronchospasm and cough and should not be used by individuals with lung disease or those who smoke."

items.statement: A higher HbA1c target (<7.5–8%) is appropriate for patients with hypoglycemia unawareness or limited life expectancy.
items.explanation: True. The target is individualized; a higher HbA1c (<7.5–8%) is appropriate for patients with cognitive impairment, reduced ability to sense hypoglycemia, or limited life span, prioritizing avoidance of hypoglycemia.
items.reference: Glycemic Control: "A higher HbA1c target of <7.5 or 8% is appropriate for individuals with cognitive impairment, those with reduced ability to sense hypoglycemia, or those with limited life span."

items.statement: In DKA, hyperglycemia typically resolves more slowly than the ketoacidosis.
items.explanation: False — it is the reverse. Hyperglycemia improves relatively quickly (4.2–5.6 mmol/L per hour), but acidosis and ketosis resolve more slowly, which is why insulin and dextrose are continued (after glucose falls to ~250) until the anion gap/bicarbonate normalize.
items.reference: DKA — Treatment: "the acidosis and ketosis resolve more slowly than hyperglycemia."

items.assertion: GLP-1 receptor agonists rarely cause hypoglycemia when used without insulin or a sulfonylurea.
items.reason: Their stimulation of insulin secretion is glucose-dependent and they suppress glucagon.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because GLP-1RAs augment insulin only when glucose is elevated (and lower glucagon), they don't drive glucose below normal unless combined with insulin/sulfonylureas.
items.reference: GLP-1RAs: "do not cause hypoglycemia because of the glucose-dependent nature of incretin-stimulated insulin secretion."

items.assertion: A patient on an SGLT-2 inhibitor can develop DKA with a near-normal blood glucose.
items.reason: Ongoing urinary glucose loss lowers blood glucose while insulin deficiency during illness still drives ketogenesis.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. The glucosuria from SGLT-2 inhibition keeps glucose down while ketoacidosis develops, producing euglycemic DKA — so ketones/acid-base must be checked even when glucose looks normal.
items.reference: SGLT-2 Inhibitors: "Euglycemic DKA may occur during illness or when ongoing glucosuria masks stress-induced requirements for insulin."

items.assertion: Insulin should be withheld in a patient with DKA whose initial potassium is 3.0 mmol/L.
items.reason: Insulin drives potassium into cells and would worsen hypokalemia, risking dangerous arrhythmias.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because insulin shifts potassium intracellularly, giving it to an already-hypokalemic patient could precipitate severe hypokalemia and arrhythmia; potassium is replaced first.
items.reference: DKA — Treatment: "If the initial serum potassium is <3.3 mmol/L... do not administer insulin until the potassium is corrected."

items.assertion: Insulin pump users must have a backup plan to inject insulin if the device fails.
items.reason: Pumps deliver only rapid-acting insulin, which has a short half-life, so an interruption rapidly leads to insulin deficiency.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. With no long-acting depot, a pump failure causes insulin levels to fall within hours and can precipitate DKA, so a backup injection plan is essential.
items.reference: Insulin pumps: "the short half-life of these insulins quickly leads to insulin deficiency if the delivery system is interrupted."

items.assertion: Very tight glycemic control is not recommended for most critically ill hospitalized patients.
items.reason: Tight control (81–108 mg/dL) increased mortality and severe hypoglycemia in the NICE-SUGAR trial.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because NICE-SUGAR showed tight control increased deaths and hypoglycemia, a moderate target (140–180 mg/dL) is recommended for most hospitalized patients.
items.reference: Hospital management: "very strict blood glucose control in acutely ill patients likely worsens outcomes and increases the frequency of hypoglycemia."

items.assertion: Metformin should be withheld before administering iodinated contrast.
items.reason: Contrast-associated kidney injury could precipitate metformin-associated lactic acidosis.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because declining renal function from contrast can lead to metformin accumulation and (rarely) lactic acidosis, metformin is held around contrast administration.
items.reference: Biguanides: "Metformin should be discontinued in... those receiving radiographic contrast material."

items.assertion: When initiating insulin in type 2 diabetes, a single bedtime dose of basal insulin is a reasonable first step.
items.reason: Increased overnight hepatic glucose production makes fasting hyperglycemia a prominent feature of type 2 DM.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because fasting hyperglycemia from hepatic glucose output dominates, bedtime basal insulin targets it and is more effective than morning dosing in trials.
items.reference: Insulin Therapy in Type 2 DM: "Because fasting hyperglycemia and increased hepatic glucose production are prominent features of type 2 DM, bedtime insulin is more effective."

items.assertion: Preconception near-normalization of HbA1c is essential in women with diabetes planning pregnancy.
items.reason: Uncontrolled diabetes at the time of conception increases the risk of fetal malformations 4–10 fold.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because organogenesis occurs soon after fertilization and hyperglycemia is teratogenic, tight preconception control (HbA1c <6.5%) markedly reduces malformation risk.
items.reference: Reproductive Issues: "The risk of fetal malformations is increased 4–10 times in individuals with uncontrolled DM at the time of conception."

items.assertion: Hypoglycemia in a patient taking an alpha-glucosidase inhibitor should be treated with pure glucose rather than sucrose.
items.reason: Alpha-glucosidase inhibitors slow the breakdown and absorption of complex carbohydrates and sucrose.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because these drugs delay digestion of sucrose/complex carbohydrates, only pure glucose is rapidly absorbed to correct hypoglycemia.
items.reference: α-Glucosidase Inhibitors: "the patient should consume glucose because the degradation and absorption of complex carbohydrates will be slowed."

items.assertion: A normal measured serum sodium in DKA indicates a more profound free-water deficit.
items.reason: Hyperglycemia osmotically draws water into the vasculature and dilutes serum sodium.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because hyperglycemia lowers measured sodium (≈1.6 mmol/L per 100 mg/dL glucose), a 'normal' sodium despite hyperglycemia implies a larger underlying water deficit.
items.reference: DKA — Laboratory: "A normal serum sodium in the setting of DKA indicates a more profound water deficit."

items.assertion: Patients with HHS have little or no ketoacidosis despite extreme hyperglycemia.
items.reason: Residual insulin in HHS is sufficient to suppress lipolysis and hepatic ketogenesis.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. The insulin deficiency in HHS is only relative — enough insulin remains to restrain ketone production, so acidosis is minimal even with massive hyperglycemia.
items.reference: HHS — Pathophysiology: "the insulin deficiency is only relative and less severe than in DKA."

items.assertion: Long-acting subcutaneous insulin should be given a few hours before stopping an intravenous insulin infusion.
items.reason: Intravenous regular insulin has a very short half-life, so an abrupt stop leaves a gap with no active insulin.
items.explanation: Both true and the reason explains the assertion. Because IV insulin clears within minutes, overlapping with subcutaneous long-acting insulin (2–4 h before stopping the drip) prevents a period of insulin deficiency and rebound hyperglycemia/DKA.
items.reference: Hospital management: "it is necessary to administer long-acting insulin prior to discontinuation of the insulin infusion (2–4 h before the infusion is stopped)."
